context.description: The passage redefines wrongdoing as an internally self-consistent justification rather than a legal or moral violation. It uses a politician thought experiment to show how self-image can drive extreme actions while preserving a sense of being good. The text argues that self-awareness is trapped in a circular self-perception that resists admitting its own failure.

rows.text_content: Wrongdoing is defined as an act that cannot be justified within oneself without creating inner contradiction. The example of bank robbery shows how a person can still see themselves as good while violating law and morality.
A politician thought experiment shows how devotion to a perceived good cause can escalate into extreme measures against imagined enemies.
The argument concludes that self-image rests on self-perception in a circular way and that admitting failure would threaten the self itself.
